title: Pruning the Path to Optimal Care: Identifying Systematically Suboptimal   Medical Decision-Making with Inverse Reinforcement Learning
authors: Inko Bovenzi, Adi Carmel, Michael Hu, Rebecca M. Hurwitz, Fiona McBride, Leo Benac, Jos\'e Roberto T...
abstract: This study used inverse reinforcement learning to identify suboptimal medical decisions by comparing clinician actions to peer consensus. Removing these suboptimal actions improved care, with benefits varying by disease and patient demographics.

title: Development and Initial Evaluation of a Patient Decision Aid to Support Decision-Making in Care-seeking Patients with Subacromial Pain Syndrome in Primary Care
authors: Kristian D Lyng *, Samantha C Bengtsen, Joshua R Zadro, Nadine E Foster, Jens L Olesen, Janus L Thom...
abstract: A patient decision aid for subacromial pain syndrome was developed and tested. Initial evaluations indicate the aid is acceptable and useful for preparing patients for decision-making.

title: Challenges for Reinforcement Learning in Healthcare
authors: Elsa Riachi, Muhammad Mamdani, Michael Fralick, Frank Rudzicz
abstract: This study explores using reinforcement learning for sequential healthcare decisions. It identifies challenges in reward specification, state representation, and policy evaluation for clinical applications.

title: Translating the Rashomon Effect to Sequential Decision-Making Tasks
authors: Dennis Gross, J{\o}rn Eirik Betten, Helge Spieker
abstract: This study investigated the Rashomon effect in sequential decision-making by comparing policies with identical behavior but different internal structures. Multiple policies exhibited the effect, and ensembles from this set improved robustness and reduced verification costs.

title: Learning-to-defer for sequential medical decision-making under   uncertainty
authors: Shalmali Joshi and Sonali Parbhoo and Finale Doshi-Velez
abstract: This study introduces a reinforcement learning framework for sequential medical decisions to account for long-term consequences. The proposed method adaptively defers to human experts, improving outcomes and reducing deferral frequency.

title: Neurophysiological insights into sequential decision-making: exploring the secretary problem through ERPs and TBR dynamics
authors: Dor Mizrahi, Ilan Laufer, Inon Zuckerman
abstract: This study investigated neurophysiological responses during a secretary problem simulation using EEG. Findings show that attention and cognitive evaluation fluctuate throughout the sequential decision-making process, with specific ERP components and TBR dynamics reflecting these changes.

title: Medical Dead-ends and Learning to Identify High-risk States and   Treatments
authors: Mehdi Fatemi, Taylor W. Killian, Jayakumar Subramanian, Marzyeh Ghassemi
abstract: This study developed a machine learning approach to identify "medical dead-ends" where patient survival is impossible. The models discovered these dead-ends in septic patients and found that administered treatments often deviated from secure options.

title: Exploring the impact of a context-adapted decision aid and online training about shared decision making about goals of care with elderly patients in the intensive care unit: a mixed-methods study
authors: Ariane Plaisance, Julien Turgeon, Lucas Gomes Souza, France Légaré C.Q., Stéphane Turcotte, Nathalie...
abstract: A mixed-methods study explored the impact of a decision aid and online training on shared decision-making for elderly ICU patients. The intervention did not significantly improve patient involvement or discussion quality, with patients often expressing confusion.

title: The Medkit-Learn(ing) Environment: Medical Decision Modelling through   Simulation
authors: Alex J. Chan, Ioana Bica, Alihan Huyuk, Daniel Jarrett, Mihaela van der Schaar
abstract: This study introduces Medkit-Learn, a Python package for simulating medical decision-making with synthetic data. It enables standardized algorithm comparison and evaluation of robustness against healthcare challenges.

title: Sequential Decision-Making for Inline Text Autocomplete
authors: Rohan Chitnis and Shentao Yang and Alborz Geramifard
abstract: This study formulates inline text autocomplete as a sequential decision-making problem using reinforcement learning to optimize suggestion policies. The approach shows potential for better suggestion policies than single-step reasoning, though real-world user alignment requires further investigation.

title: Counterfactually Guided Off-policy Transfer in Clinical Settings
authors: Taylor W. Killian, Marzyeh Ghassemi, Shalmali Joshi
abstract: This study developed a causal modeling approach to transfer clinical decision policies to new patient populations with limited data. The method effectively improved treatment policy performance by using counterfactual trajectories and informative priors, even with unobserved confounding.

title: Training Undergraduate Medical Students in Shared Decision Making: A Systematic Review
authors: Saurabh RamBihariLal Shrivastava, Prateek Saurabh Shrivastava, Nandkishor Bankar, Gulshan Bandre, Va...
abstract: This systematic review explored shared decision-making (SDM) training for undergraduate medical students. Effective SDM integration requires a multipronged approach including curriculum changes and assessment methods.

title: A Conservative Q-Learning approach for handling distribution shift in   sepsis treatment strategies
authors: Pramod Kaushik, Sneha Kummetha, Perusha Moodley, Raju S. Bapi
abstract: This study used Conservative Q-Learning to address distribution shift in offline reinforcement learning for sepsis treatment. The developed policy more closely matched physician actions than standard methods, potentially improving patient outcomes.

title: Sequential Decision Making with Expert Demonstrations under Unobserved Heterogeneity
authors: Vahid Balazadeh, Keertana Chidambaram, Viet Nguyen, Rahul G. Krishnan, Vasilis Syrgkanis
abstract: This study addresses sequential decision-making using expert demonstrations with unobserved context by modeling it as meta-reinforcement learning. The proposed Experts-as-Priors algorithm effectively uses expert data to inform the learner's prior, outperforming existing methods.

title: Intensive Care as One Big Sequence Modeling Problem
authors: Vadim Liventsev, Tobias Fritz
abstract: This study frames intensive care as a sequence modeling problem, representing patient-provider interactions as event streams. The MIMIC-SEQ benchmark was developed to train models for predicting future clinical events.

title: Noise is an underrecognized problem in medical decision making and is known by other names: a scoping review
authors: Kayla V. Dlugos, Mjaye Mazwi, Robert Lao, Osami Honjo
abstract: This scoping review explored noise, or random variability, in medical decision making. The analysis suggests noise drives unhelpful practice variation and impacts care efficiency and reproducibility.

title: Offline reinforcement learning with uncertainty for treatment strategies   in sepsis
authors: Ran Liu (1 and 2), Joseph L. Greenstein (1 and 2), James C. Fackler (3), Jules Bergmann (3), Melania...
abstract: This study used reinforcement learning to find optimal sepsis treatment strategies from data and estimate their confidence. The method identified biases against aggressive treatment and developed a way to mitigate them for more accurate policies.

title: Family satisfaction with involvement in decision making in the intensive care unit: A scoping review
authors: Ntombifikile Klaas *, Onalenna Baliki
abstract: This scoping review identified literature on family satisfaction with ICU decision-making involvement. Effective communication and support are crucial for enhancing family satisfaction and involvement in these decisions.

title: Empowering Clinicians with Medical Decision Transformers: A Framework   for Sepsis Treatment
authors: Aamer Abdul Rahman, Pranav Agarwal, Rita Noumeir, Philippe Jouvet, Vincent Michalski, Samira Ebrahim...
abstract: This study developed a medical decision transformer (MeDT) framework to improve sepsis treatment recommendations. MeDT provides interpretable, personalized, and clinician-directed interventions that are competitive with existing methods.

title: Active Sampling for MRI-based Sequential Decision Making
authors: Yuning Du, Jingshuai Liu, Rohan Dharmakumar, Sotirios A. Tsaftaris
abstract: This study developed a reinforcement learning framework to optimize MRI data acquisition for sequential diagnostic decisions. The approach achieved competitive diagnostic performance while significantly reducing the number of k-space samples needed.
